summary: Spolsky recounts a frustrating experience at Hertz DFW airport where a computer system failure brought the entire rental operation to a halt. With employees forced to fill out agreements by hand and managers sitting idle, the cascading failure left him stranded in suburban Dallas without transportation. He speculates that the entire outage might have been caused by something as simple as a DOS prompt waiting for a date entry. The post illustrates how over-reliance on automated systems without adequate fallback procedures creates spectacular failures.
key_points: Hertz's automated systems failed completely, and the company lacked enough trained staff to handle manual operations as fallback
The cascading failure spread beyond Hertz as displaced customers booked out every other rental agency at the airport
Technology-driven staffing reductions create brittleness: the fancy computers justified fewer employees, but left no capacity for when those computers failed
Simple technical problems (like a DOS box waiting for a date input) can bring down entire operations when no one present understands the underlying systems
money_quotes: Thanks to the spiffy new computers, they don't have nearly enough people working there to handle the loads when the computers break down.
There were still three Hertz employees sitting around in the manager's office shooting the breeze despite the lines of angry customers out the door.

If there was just one person working for Hertz who could actually read, that person would have typed in the date on time on that old DOS box, which then would have booted up and the whole system would have come back.
We're stuck in the middle of Addison, north of Dallas, without a car, in the least pedestrian-friendly spot of the known universe.
I think I'm going to have to buy a car, because Southwest Airlines is booked solid all day. I guess in a pinch I can always charter a jet ($4673).
key_insight: Automating operations without maintaining fallback capacity and human competence creates systems that fail catastrophically rather than gracefully degrading.
